channel.title: Political Science, History, and Statistics on Andre Walter
channel.description: Recent content in Political Science, History, and Statistics on Andre Walter

item.title: About
item.description: I’m a research assistant at the Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen. My areas of interest are comparative political economy, comparative politics and statistical methods. In my PhD project, I investigate the effect of electoral competition, interstate cooperation and the interaction of direct democracy and coalition governments on social policies and social expenditure. In addition, I have been involved in data collections about the impact of colonialism in Africa and Asia and the legislative and executive composition in the Swiss Cantons.

item.title: CV
item.description: Academic Employment 2012– Research Assistant, Chair for Comparative Political Economy and Public Policy, Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland. 2011–2012 Teaching Assistant, Gender Studies, University of Zurich, Switzerland. 2009–2010 Student and Teaching Assistant, Institute of Sociology, University of Jena, Germany. Education 2017 PhD, Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland 2012 MA, Sociology, Social-/Cultural Anthropology, and Gender Studies, University of Zurich, Switzerland 2010 BA, Sociology and Political Science, University of Jena, Germany Additional Training 2017

item.title: Publications
item.description: Unter Review A Race to the Top. The Politics of Interstate Cost Distribution and Welfare State Expansion A Disproportional Threat. The Adoption of Proportional Representation in Switzerland (with Patrick Emmenegger) Working Papers Internal Migration and the Expansion of Health Insurance Coverage [Working Title] Taking the Initiative! Direct Democracy, Coalition Governments and Welfare State Expansion Work in Progress The Adoption of Proportional Representation in the Swiss Cantons (with Patrick Emmenegger) The Adoption of Personal Income Taxation in the Swiss Cantons [Working Title] (with Patrick Emmenegger and Lucas Leemann) An extended Dataset for the Executive and Legislative Composition of the Swiss Cantons in the 19th and 20th Century [Working Title] (with Patrick Emmenegger) The Abolition of Feudal Taxation across Europe [Working Title] Electoral Competition and the Mobilization of the Catholic Workers’ Movement [Working Title] A Socialist Threat?

item.title: Swiss Cantonal Governments 1848-2017
item.description: I’m currently working with Patrick Emmenegger on creating a database on the ideological composition of cantonal governments. We were able to compile a time series for most cantons starting in the mid 19th century. A complete Time-Series Cross-Section is available for all cantons for 1894 onwards (except Appenzell Inner-Rhodes which starts 1928 and, of course, Jura). Below, you can find the first exploratory results. The first plot displays the smoothed government volatility for 24 cantons (excl.

item.title: Teaching
item.description: 2018 The Politics of Electoral Systems: Political Conflict and Representation, Seminar for MA Students, University of St. Gallen (Spring Term) 2017 Causal Arguments and Analysis: Guest lecture invited by Prof. Patrick Emmenegger, University of St. Gallen (Spring Term) 2012-2016 (Teaching Assistant at the University of St. Gallen) Qualitative Methods (Prof. James Davis/Prof. Klaus Dingwerth) Quantitative Methods (Prof. Daniele Caramani/Prof. Oliver Westerwinter) Research Design (Prof. Patrick Emmenegger) QCA (Prof.

item.title: Estimation of Long Run Effects in R
item.description: A lot of quantitative data in political science comes in the form of “Time-Series Cross-Sectional” (TSCS) datasets. Such datasets usually consist of a low number of cross-sectional units (e.g. states, countries) and higher number of time periods. Since the introduction of panel-corrected standard errors in political science in 1995 by Beck and Katz, political science scholars have discussed the merits and pitfalls of TSCS data. One controversial aspect is the inclusion of lagged dependent variables (LDVs) in estimation equations.
